name: Nothung
description: Nothung, also known as Balmung or Gram, is the legendary sword wielded by the hero Sigmund in the Volsunga Saga and later by his son, Sigurd (Siegfried). Forged by the mythical smith Völundr (Wayland), the sword was initially embedded in the Barnstokkr tree in King Volsung's hall. Only Sigmund was strong enough to pull it free, proving his worth and lineage. Nothung possessed immense strength and sharpness, capable of cleaving through anything, including anvils. Its power was intrinsically linked to the Volsung bloodline, symbolizing their heroic destiny and tragic fate. After Sigmund's death in battle, the sword was shattered by Odin's spear, Gungnir. The fragments were then reforged by Regin for Sigurd, who used it to slay the dragon Fafnir and claim the treasure hoard. The sword ultimately plays a crucial role in the events leading to Sigurd's own demise, making it a potent symbol of both heroic power and the inevitability of fate in Norse mythology. Its creation by a master smith, its unique properties, and its connection to a lineage of great heroes makes it one of the most iconic weapons in Norse lore. The sword is a symbol of destiny, power, and the tragic cycle of violence and revenge.
subtitle: The Sword of the Volsungs' Fate
origin: Forged by the smith Völundr and embedded in the Barnstokkr tree by Odin.
currentLocation: Lost to time, potentially in a dragon's hoard or buried with a hero.

keyMyths.title: The Sword in the Tree
keyMyths.description: Odin plunged Nothung into the Barnstokkr tree. Only Sigmund was able to draw it out, marking him as a true hero.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Fafnir
keyMyths.description: Sigurd used Nothung, reforged from its fragments, to slay the dragon Fafnir and claim the cursed treasure hoard.

sources.author: Unknown
sources.description: The primary source for the story of Nothung and the Volsung family.
